title: Linked Lists: Boxes with Arrows
subtitle: Data without a fixed seating chart

sections.heading: A linked list is different from an array
sections.body: Arrays sit next to each other in memory. Linked list nodes don't — they live anywhere, but each one carries a pointer to the next one. To get to position 5, you have to *walk* from the start, following arrows.

sections.title: A train of cars
sections.body: Picture a train. Each car holds cargo and a hook connecting to the next car. To reach the caboose, you walk car by car. You can easily add or remove cars in the middle — just unhook and re-hook. But there's no 'jump to car 12'. You walk.

sections.caption: Each node holds a value and a pointer to the next.

sections.heading: Trade-offs
sections.body:
**Insert/delete at the front:** O(1). Just re-point one arrow.
**Access by index:** O(n). You have to walk.

Use a linked list when you do lots of inserting and removing at the ends. Use an array when you need fast random access.

questions.question: What's the time complexity of getting the 50th element of a linked list?
questions.explanation: You must walk from the head, following 50 pointers — that's linear time.

questions.question: What does the LAST node in a singly linked list point to?
questions.explanation: By convention, the last node's `next` is null — signaling end of list.
